quality.rationale: 需结合瞬时静止系洛伦兹变换、协变守恒方程与球坐标δ函数积分，逻辑链条完整且具相对论运动学背景。

origin.question: Problem 5.7. An infinitesimally thin rod of length $2a$ has a point mass $m$ at each of its ends. The center of the rod is fixed in the laboratory and the rod rotates about this point with a relativistic angular velocity $\omega$ . (i.e. $\omega \ell$ is comparable with $c$ ). Assume the rod is massless. What is $\mathbf{T}^{\mu \nu}$ for the rod and particle system?
origin.answer: $\mathbf{T}^{\mu \nu} = \frac{\mathfrak{m}}{1 - \omega^{2} \mathfrak{a}^{2}} \delta(\cos \theta) \left[ \delta(\phi - \omega \mathfrak{t}) + \delta(\phi - \omega \mathfrak{t} - \pi)\right] \times \left[ \begin{array}{c c c c} \frac{\delta(r - a)}{a^{2}} & 0 & 0 & \frac{\omega \delta(r - a)}{a^{2}} \\ & -\frac{\omega^{2} a}{r^{2}} & 0 & 0 \\ & & 0 & 0 \\ \text{symmetric} & & & \frac{\omega^{2} \delta(r - a)}{a^{2}} \end{array}\right]$
origin.solution:
Solution 5.7. Let $S'$ be the instantaneous rest frame of an element of![](images/c7c1955c9b6e9be687989cc12e1d092f09ebea30b9a64a114595543f389eb2f7.jpg)the rod. In this rest frame the only nonzero components are $\mathbf{T}^{0'0'} = ho$ , $\mathbf{T}^{x'x'} = \mathfrak{p}$ . If we Lorentz transform to the lab frame we find the nonzero components: $\mathbf{T}^{xx} = \mathfrak{p}$ , $\mathbf{T}^{yy} = \gamma^{2} eta^{2}ho$ , $\mathbf{T}^{00} = \gamma^{2}ho$ , $\mathbf{T}^{0y} = \gamma^{2} etaho$ , where $ eta = \omega r$ and $\gamma \equiv (1 -  eta^{2})^{- rac{1}{2}}$ . If spherical polar coordinates are used the nonzero components are$$\mathbf{T}^{rr} = \mathfrak{p}, \quad \mathbf{T}^{\phi\phi} = \gamma^{2}  eta^{2} ho / r^{2}, \quad \mathbf{T}^{0\phi} = \gamma^{2}  eta ho / r, \quad \mathbf{T}^{00} = \gamma^{2} ho .$$To find $\mathfrak{p}(r)$ we can use the $r$ component of the equation of motion$$ egin{array}{l}0 = \mathrm{T}^{r
u}_{;
u} = \mathrm{T}^{rr}_{,r} + \mathrm{T}^{\phi\phi}\Gamma_{\phi\phi}^{r} + \mathrm{T}^{rr}\left[\log(-g)^{ rac{1}{2}}ight]_{,r} \\mathbf{	au} = \left(\mathrm{T}^{rr} r^{2}ight)_{,r} / r^{2} - r\sin^{2}	heta \mathrm{T}^{\phi\phi}\end{array}$$so that$$\left(\operatorname{pr}^{2}ight)_{,r} = r\sin^{2}	heta \gamma^{2} eta^{2}ho .$$Since the only mass density is in the point masses we have$$ho = m\delta(r - a)\delta(\cos	heta)[\delta(\phi - \omega t) + \delta(\phi - \omega t - \pi)] / r^{2}.$$We can now integrate the equation of motion with the boundary condition $\mathfrak{p} = 0$ for $r > a$ , to find $\mathfrak{p}$ . When this is done the components of the stress-energy tensor (in the lab, described by coordinates $t, r, 	heta, \phi$ ) are seen to be$$ egin{array}{l}\mathbf{T}^{\mu 
u} =  rac{\mathfrak{m}}{1 - \omega^{2} \mathfrak{a}^{2}} \delta(\cos 	heta) \left[ \delta(\phi - \omega \mathfrak{t}) + \delta(\phi - \omega \mathfrak{t} - \pi)ight] \	imes \left[  egin{array}{c c c c}  rac{\delta(r - a)}{a^{2}} & 0 & 0 &  rac{\omega \delta(r - a)}{a^{2}} \ & - rac{\omega^{2} a}{r^{2}} & 0 & 0 \ & & 0 & 0 \
	ext{symmetric} & & &  rac{\omega^{2} \delta(r - a)}{a^{2}} \end{array}ight]\end{array}$$
